title: Shared and Slow
subtitle: The dial-up era and the birth of broadband in Singapore
overview: The 2000s were defined by slow, shared internet connections. Most households started the decade with dial-up modems that tied up phone lines and delivered speeds of just 56 Kbps. By the end of the decade, ADSL broadband had become common, but speeds were still measured in single-digit megabits. For gamers, this meant high ping, frequent disconnections, and a reliance on cybercafés for any serious online play.

timeline.description: Most Singaporean households used 56k dial-up modems. Internet was slow, expensive per minute, and shared the phone line.
Singtel launched ADSL broadband services offering 'always-on' internet at speeds up to 512 Kbps.
StarHub entered the market with cable internet, competing with Singtel and pushing speeds to 1-2 Mbps.
The majority of households switched from dial-up to ADSL. Speeds reached 3-6 Mbps.
IDA announced the Next Generation National Broadband Network (NGNBN), setting the stage for fibre.

gamingDescription: Online gaming in the 2000s was challenging due to slow speeds and high latency. Most serious gamers frequented cybercafés, which had faster connections and lower ping to game servers.
gamingHighlights: Cybercafés were the primary place for competitive gaming
Popular games: Counter-Strike 1.6, StarCraft: Brood War, MapleStory
LAN parties were common since home internet was unreliable
High ping (200-300ms) made fast-paced games difficult to play
challenges: Shared phone lines meant someone picking up the phone would disconnect the internet
Download speeds were painfully slow - a 5MB file could take 10+ minutes
Monthly data caps were common and expensive to exceed
Latency made online gaming frustrating, especially for competitive play
